agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider 2025 Homeless Count report for Vancouver
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council will consider a report and presentation on the 2025 Homeless Count for Vancouver. This is a proposed review of the data, not a final decision. The count tracks how many people are experiencing homelessness in the region. Council may use this information to guide future decisions on housing and support services. No bylaw or rule change is being voted on at this time.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
1.	2025 Homeless Count – Vancouver
(no speakers per section 7.2(d) of the Procedure By-law)
Report
Presentation
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2026 Cultural Grants for Operating & Spaces
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on proposed 2026 Cultural Grants that would provide funding for arts and culture organizations in Burnaby. The grants cover two areas: operating costs and cultural spaces. Some parts of the proposal need at least two-thirds of all council members to vote 'yes' — this is a special rule from the Vancouver Charter (the law that sets how the city runs). The decision has not been made yet; council will discuss and vote on it at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and consider contacting your council member to share your views on the proposed grants.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
2.	2026 Cultural Grants (Balance of Operating, Cultural Spaces)
Recommendations A to C, E and G require at least 2/3 affirmative votes of all Council members under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants for Spring 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to award grants to community sport events happening in Vancouver during the Spring of 2026. This is part of the Vancouver Community Sport Event Grant program. The recommendation requires at least two-thirds of all council members to vote 'yes' to pass, as per Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter (a special rule for certain council decisions). If approved, the grants would help fund local sporting events that bring people together.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you run a community sport event in Vancouver, watch for the council decision — this could mean grant funding for your Spring 2026 event.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
3.	Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants – Spring 2026 Intake
Recommendation A requires at least 2/3 affirmative votes of all Council members under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby property tax distribution for 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on how to divide the total property tax levy among different classes of properties (like homes, businesses, and industry) for the 2026 tax year. This decision will determine how much of the overall tax burden falls on commercial and residential property owners. A proposed bylaw would set the tax rates for each property class. The outcome directly affects how much property tax your business will pay next year.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this will set your 2026 property tax rate.
agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Professional Services
agenda_briefs.source_body:
4.	2026 Property Taxation: Distribution of Property Tax Levy
4a. Budget Resolution
4b. Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2025 property tax exemptions for non-profits
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a report that recommends granting property tax exemptions for 2025 to certain non-profit organizations in Burnaby. A property tax exemption means the organization does not have to pay property tax on the land or building it owns or leases. This decision would affect which groups get the break and how much tax revenue the city gives up. The report is on the agenda for an upcoming council meeting and has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action required from you at this time. Watch for the council decision to see if your property or business is affected.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
5.	2025 Property Tax Exemptions
(no speakers per section 7.2(d) of the Procedure By-law)
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Coastal Flood Management Policy
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed Coastal Flood Management Policy. This policy would create new rules for building and development in areas of Burnaby that are at risk of flooding from the ocean. If approved, property owners in these flood-risk zones may face new requirements for building elevations, flood-proofing, and insurance. The goal is to protect people and property from rising sea levels and storm surges. The policy is still a proposal and has not been finalized.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Check if your property is in a proposed flood-risk zone and review the draft policy before the council vote.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Insurance
agenda_briefs.source_body:
6.	Coastal Flood Management Policy
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider Vancouver Heritage Foundation Annual Report 2025
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a recommendation (Recommendation B) regarding the Vancouver Heritage Foundation's 2025 Annual Report. This recommendation requires at least two-thirds of all council members to vote in favour, as stated under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter. The decision is about whether to accept or act on the report's findings or proposals. The outcome could affect heritage properties and related programs in Vancouver.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Professional Services
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.	Vancouver Heritage Foundation Board – Annual Report 2025
Recommendation B requires at least 2/3 affirmative votes of all Council members under Section 206(1) of theVancouver Charter
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on public plaza alcohol sites for 2026-2029
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to choose which public plazas in Burnaby will allow people to drink alcohol in public from 2026 to 2029. This is part of the Alcohol Consumption in Public Plazas Program. If approved, the program would let adults drink alcohol in certain outdoor public spaces during specific hours and dates. The decision has not been made yet — council will discuss and vote on the list of sites at an upcoming meeting. Business owners near proposed plazas should pay attention, as this could affect foot traffic, noise, and local business operations.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and consider contacting your city councillor if you have concerns about a plaza near your business.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.	Sites for 2026-2029 Alcohol Consumption in Public Plazas Program
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to apply for development approvals funding
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on whether to apply for a provincial grant through the Local Government Development Approvals Program. If approved, the funding would help the city speed up its review and approval process for new development projects. This could mean faster decisions on building permits and rezoning applications for local businesses and developers. The decision has not been made yet — council will discuss and vote on the application at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if approved, this could speed up your future development applications in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.	Funding Application to Local Government Development Approvals Program
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on housing rezoning rule changes
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a package of miscellaneous amendments that would change the conditions attached to various housing rezoning approvals. A proposed bylaw would update standard requirements for things like affordable housing units, parking, and community amenity contributions. These changes would apply to future rezoning applications, not projects already approved. The report is on the agenda for the next council meeting, but no decision has been made yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if passed, these new conditions will apply to any future rezoning application you submit.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
10.	Miscellaneous Amendments Concerning Various Housing Rezoning Conditions
(no speakers per section 7.2(b) of the Procedure By-law)
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Text amendment to CD-1 (839) bylaw for 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed text amendment to the Comprehensive Development (CD-1) Bylaw No. 13493 for properties at 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue. A CD-1 bylaw is a special zoning rule that allows a specific development plan for a site. This amendment would change the rules for that site. The decision has not been made yet — it is up for approval at a future council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect development rules for these properties.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: CD-1 Rezoning at 4088 Granville Street
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed rezoning bylaw for 4088 Granville Street. The draft bylaw would change the zoning rules for this property, allowing a different type of development than what is currently permitted. This is a first reading of the bylaw — it has not been approved yet. The decision is on the agenda for an upcoming council meeting.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning for 3803-3823 West 10th Ave and 2553 Highbury St
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed rezoning for properties at 3803-3823 West 10th Avenue and 2553 Highbury Street. The plan would change the land-use rules (called a CD-1 rezoning) to allow a new development on these sites. A draft bylaw to make this change is up for approval at the next meeting. This is a proposal only — it has not been approved yet.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: CD-1 Rezoning for 310 East 14th Avenue in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone the property at 310 East 14th Avenue. This is called a Comprehensive Development (CD-1) rezoning, which means the city would create a special set of rules just for this piece of land, instead of following the standard zoning rules. The draft bylaw would amend Burnaby's Zoning and Development Bylaw. A decision has not been made yet — this item is on the council meeting agenda for discussion and a vote.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No immediate action required. Watch for the council decision — this could affect nearby property values or development plans.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning at 1305-1325 West 13th Avenue in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone the properties at 1305-1325 West 13th Avenue. This is a draft bylaw to amend the city's Zoning and Development Bylaw. If approved, the rezoning would change what can be built or done on these properties. The decision has not been made yet — this is an upcoming proposal on the council agenda.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this rezoning could affect nearby property values or development plans.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby amenity cost charge bylaw and density bonus updates
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to consider proposed updates to the Amenity Cost Charge bylaw and the Density Bonus and Inclusionary Zoning policies. An amenity cost charge is a fee the city can collect from new developments to help pay for community amenities like parks, community centres, and libraries. A density bonus allows developers to build more units in exchange for providing affordable housing or other community benefits. The report outlines required public consultation before these changes can be approved. If passed, these updates could affect how much developers pay and what community benefits they must provide in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for public consultation dates — you may have a chance to comment on the proposed bylaw changes.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
16.	2026 Financing Growth Update – Required Consultation for Amenity Cost Charge By law and Bill 16 Density Bonus and Inclusionary Zoning Updates
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw to assess property for Trounce Alley and Blood Alley maintenance
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would charge property owners in the Trounce Alley and Blood Alley Square area for the 2025 maintenance costs of those public spaces. A 'local improvement project' is a way for a city to pay for upgrades or upkeep in a specific area by billing the properties that benefit from the work. If approved, this bylaw would mean owners of affected properties would have to pay a share of the maintenance costs for the coming year.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Check if your property is in the Trounce Alley or Blood Alley Square area and watch for the council vote.
agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Food Service
agenda_briefs.source_body: 8.	A By-law to assess real property to defray 2025 maintenance costs for the Trounce Alley and Blood Alley Square Local Improvement Project
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Bylaw to assess property for East Hastings parking project costs
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would assess (charge) certain properties to help pay for the 2025 costs of the East Hastings Street Collective Parking Project. This means some property owners in the area could be required to pay a special levy to fund shared parking improvements. The decision has not been made yet — it is up for approval at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if passed, this bylaw could mean a new charge on your property tax bill.
agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Construction & Development
agenda_briefs.source_body: 7.	A By-law to assess real property to defray 2025 costs for the East Hastings Street Collective Parking Project
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Housing agreement for 320-360 West 2nd Avenue up for council vote
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would create a Housing Agreement for the property at 320-360 West 2nd Avenue. A Housing Agreement is a legal contract between the city and a property owner that locks in how the building will be used — in this case, to ensure the units remain as affordable rental housing for a set number of years. This is a proposed decision only; it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.source_body: 6.	A By-law to enact a Housing Agreement for 320-360 West 2nd Avenue
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Amend housing agreement for 450-496 Prior Street, 550 Malkin Ave, 1002 Station St
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would amend an existing Housing Agreement for three properties in Burnaby: 450-496 Prior Street, 550 Malkin Avenue, and 1002 Station Street. A Housing Agreement is a legal contract between the city and a property owner that sets rules for how the property can be used, often to ensure affordable housing or specific rental terms. This proposed change would update the terms of that agreement. The decision has not been made yet.
agenda_briefs.source_body: 5.	A By-law to authorize the amendment of a Housing Agreement Authorized by By-law No. 14507 (450 – 496 Prior Street, 550 Malkin Avenue and 1002 Station Street)
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Amend CD-1 (198) bylaw for 1700-1750 West 75th Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would change the rules for a specific property at 1700-1750 West 75th Avenue. This property is currently zoned under a 'Comprehensive Development' (CD-1) bylaw, which is a special set of rules for that site. The proposed amendment would update or change those rules. The decision has not been made yet — Council will discuss and vote on it at an upcoming meeting. Councillor Montague must review the meeting proceedings before being allowed to vote on this item.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and attend or submit comments if you own or operate a business at this location.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
4.	A By-law to amend CD-1 (198) By-law No. 6254 (1700-1750 West 75th Avenue)
(Councillor Montague will need to review the proceedings to be eligible to vote)
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezone 1710-1730 East Pender Street to CD-1
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone the properties at 1710-1730 East Pender Street to a Comprehensive Development (CD-1) zone. A CD-1 zone is a custom zoning category that allows for a specific development plan, rather than following the standard rules for that area. This is a first reading of the bylaw — it has not been approved yet. Councillors Maloney, Orr and the Mayor must review the proceedings before they are eligible to vote on it.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — this is your chance to have a say on the proposed rezoning.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
3.	A By-law to amend Zoning and Development By-law No. 3575 to rezone an area to CD-1 (1710-1730 East Pender Street)
(Councillor Maloney, Orr and Mayor Sim will need to review the proceedings to be eligible to vote)
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezone 6486 Chester Street to CD-1 in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone the property at 6486 Chester Street to a Comprehensive Development (CD-1) zone. A CD-1 zone is a custom zoning category created for a specific development project, rather than following standard zoning rules. This means the land could be used for a particular type of building or business that wouldn't normally be allowed under the current zoning. The decision has not been made yet — Council will consider the proposal at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and attend the public hearing to have your say on this rezoning proposal.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
2.	A By-law to amend Zoning and Development By-law No. 3575 to rezone an area to CD-1 (6486 Chester Street)
(Councillors Bligh, Fry and Mayor Sim will need to review the proceedings to be eligible to vote)
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone the properties at 1302-1318 East 12th Avenue to a Comprehensive Development (CD-1) zone. A CD-1 zone is a custom zoning category that allows for a specific development plan, rather than following standard zoning rules. This is a proposed change only — it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Attend the public hearing or submit written comments to Burnaby Council before the vote to share your support or concerns.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
1.	A By-law to amend Zoning and Development By-law No. 3575 to rezone an area to CD-1 (1302 - 1318 East 12th Avenue)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: CD-1 Rezoning for 888 West 8th Avenue in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed rezoning for the property at 888 West 8th Avenue. A 'CD-1' (Comprehensive Development) zone would replace the current zoning, allowing a specific development plan for this site. This is a referral report, meaning council is considering whether to move forward with public hearings and further review. The decision has not been made yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — this is your chance to comment on the proposed rezoning.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
2.	CD-1 Rezoning: 888 West 8th Avenue

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Fast-track rental housing and small hotels in new Burnaby mixed-use zone
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposal to create a new 'Mixed-Use Commercial District' that would fast-track approvals for rental housing and small hotels. If approved, this new zoning category would allow developers to build rental apartments and small hotels more quickly in certain commercial areas, without needing a full rezoning process each time. The goal is to speed up the construction of much-needed rental homes and tourist accommodations. Council will consider the referral report at an upcoming meeting before any final decision is made.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and consider attending or submitting comments if you own property in a commercial area that could be rezoned.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Hotel & Accommodation, Landlord & Property Mgmt
agenda_briefs.source_body:
1.	Fast-Tracking Rental Housing and Small Hotels – New Mixed-Use Commercial District
Referral Report
